prd.summary: CampusHub consolidates campus events and study-group matching for commuter students. MVP tests whether commuters will adopt a unified platform over fragmented channels.
prd.problem_statement: Commuter students today learn about campus events through fragmented channels (email, flyers, multiple apps) and frequently miss them. They lack a systematic way to find study partners before leaving campus.

target_user.who: Commuter students at large public universities who spend 4+ hours on campus per week
target_user.situation: Between classes or during study blocks, checking their phone for events and peer connection opportunities

in_scope.item: Personalized events feed (campus-hosted events only, no third-party integration)
in_scope.rationale: Directly addresses the riskiest assumption: will students open a new app for campus information? Events are the highest-frequency use case and easiest to validate in 8–10 weeks with a 2–3 person team.

in_scope.item: Study-group matching by course and schedule
in_scope.rationale: Tests secondary adoption driver without requiring marketplace infrastructure. Matching algorithm can be rule-based (no ML). Validates whether students will use the app for peer connection.

in_scope.item: Basic user onboarding (course roster import or manual entry)
in_scope.rationale: Minimal viable gate-keeping. Manual entry keeps backend simple; course roster integration deferred. Required to power both in-scope features.

out_of_scope.reason: Requires vendor trust, payment processing, and moderation at scale. A 2–3 person team cannot ship marketplace + events + matching in one cycle.
out_of_scope.revisit_when: Marketplace demand is validated through in-app survey or 40%+ of weekly active users request it

out_of_scope.reason: Requires restaurant/vendor partnerships, logistics coordination, and payment integration. Out of scope for team size and timeline.
out_of_scope.revisit_when: Post-launch, if food-related feature requests exceed 20% of feedback

out_of_scope.reason: Moderation burden and community-building complexity exceed team capacity. Forum success depends on critical mass; premature launch risks a ghost feature.
out_of_scope.revisit_when: After events and matching reach 15%+ weekly active user penetration and team expands

out_of_scope.reason: One platform ships in 8–10 weeks with 2–3 people. Second platform is a post-launch priority.
out_of_scope.revisit_when: First platform reaches 20% weekly active user target

success_metrics.metric: Weekly active commuter students (% of enrolled commuters at pilot campus)
Events feed open rate (% of weekly active users who view ≥1 event)
Study-group match acceptance rate (% of matches that result in ≥1 confirmed meeting)

risks.risk: Course roster import unavailable; manual entry friction causes low onboarding completion.
risks.mitigation: Validate course import feasibility with IT department in week 1. If unavailable, design 60-second manual entry flow and measure completion rate by day 3.

risks.risk: Pilot campus has low event volume or events already well-promoted; feed provides no new value.
risks.mitigation: Audit campus event calendar before launch. If <5 events/week, partner with student orgs to create recurring events to seed the feed.

risks.risk: Study-group matching produces low-quality or no matches due to sparse course overlap in pilot cohort.
risks.mitigation: Validate course enrollment distribution in pilot segment. If <30% of users have course overlap, defer matching to post-launch and replace with manual bulletin board.

risks.risk: Students perceive the app as redundant and do not install; adoption stalls below 5% by week 4.
risks.mitigation: Weekly cohort analysis of install-to-first-open ratio. If <40% of installers open the app by day 3, pause marketing and conduct 5 user interviews to diagnose friction.

open_questions: Which campus will be the pilot, and what is the total commuter enrollment?
Does campus IT allow third-party apps to import course rosters, and what is the integration timeline?
What is the iOS vs. Android usage breakdown among target commuters at the pilot campus?
next_release: Used textbook and furniture marketplace (post-validation of events + matching adoption)
Campus food pickup and delivery (post-launch, if demand signals warrant)
Second platform (iOS or Android, whichever was not in MVP)

flags.issue: Current state of event discovery not specified; unclear what students do today.
flags.why_it_matters: Without knowing whether they check email daily, scroll Instagram, or ignore events entirely, you cannot design a feed that actually changes behavior.
flags.suggested_fix: Rewrite problem to state: 'Commuters today learn about events via [email/Instagram/GroupMe/flyers]; 31 of 40 surveyed miss events because [too many channels / information arrives too late].'

flags.issue: Primary metric is measured at end of quarter; no early warning signal if adoption stalls.
flags.why_it_matters: If week-1 install rate is 2%, you will not know until week 12, leaving no time to pivot or diagnose.
flags.suggested_fix: Add a week-1 or week-2 checkpoint: 'Install rate ≥10% of target commuters by end of week 1' as a go/no-go gate.

flags.issue: Platform choice ('both') is deferred in MVP but not explicitly stated as a constraint trade-off.
flags.why_it_matters: Committing to iOS and Android in parallel with a 2–3 person team will delay launch or force cuts elsewhere.
flags.suggested_fix: Explicitly choose one platform for MVP (e.g., 'iOS, based on 65% iOS penetration in pilot segment') and move the second to next-release.

flags.issue: No risk for low event volume or stale feed; if campus has few events, the feed is empty and worthless.
flags.why_it_matters: An empty events feed is a retention killer and will be discovered in week 1 of testing.
flags.suggested_fix: Add a pre-launch audit: confirm the pilot campus has ≥5 events/week and a plan to seed the feed if it does not.

critique.strongest_element: Riskiest assumption is explicit and well-chosen; the MVP design directly tests it (events feed as the primary adoption lever) rather than building all five features and hoping one sticks.
critique.weakest_element: Success metric lacks an early-stage checkpoint and the problem statement never describes what commuters do today to find events, making it impossible to know whether the app actually solves the stated problem or just adds another channel to an already-fragmented landscape.
